title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text: This assignment recreates the Indego assessment from the in-class assignment and aligns it with the second-quarter data. It then develops a basic understanding of demand patterns in the second quarter and compares the model results. The assignment takes the analysis a step further by adding spatial features and testing potential improvements. Finally, it concludes with an equity-focused analysis to help inform policy-level decisions.

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
Coding and Replicating
We selected Q2 2025 (April–June) for this homework because it represents Philadelphia’s prime biking season: temperatures rise, daylight extends, and ridership typically surges relative to Q1’s winter baseline. This makes Q2 a compelling contrast case, the same model framework applied to a completely different demand regime.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(sf)
library(tigris)
library(tidycensus)
library(riem)  # For Philadelphia weather from ASOS stations

library(viridis) # color scales for visualization
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(patchwork)


library(here)
options(scipen = 999) # Get rid of scientific notation to make it look tidier

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
options(tigris_progress = FALSE)


Defining reusable plot and map themes for consistent ggplot2 styling, plus a 5-color palette.


Code
# plotTheme: reusable ggplot2 theme for charts and graphs 
plotTheme1 <- theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(size = 10, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    axis.text.y   = element_text(size = 10),
    axis.title    = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "#D0D0D0", linewidth = 0.2),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks    = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  )

# mapTheme: reusable ggplot2 theme for spatial/map plots 
mapTheme1 <- theme_void() +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size = 8),
    legend.position  = "right",
    plot.margin      = margin(1, 1, 1, 1, "cm"),
    legend.key.height = unit(1, "cm"),
    legend.key.width  = unit(0.2, "cm")
  )

#palette5: 5-color palette for categorical or sequential mapping
palette51 <- c("lightpink", "red", "lightblue", "darkblue", "green")

Aggregating trips into hourly bins.


Code
# Clean up the dates and add useful time columns
indego1 <- indego1 %>%
  mutate(
    # Parse datetime
    start_datetime = mdy_hm(start_time),
    end_datetime = mdy_hm(end_time),
    
    # Create hourly bins
    interval601 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"),
    
    # Extract time features
    week = week(interval601),
    month = month(interval601, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval601, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval601),
    date = as.Date(interval601),
    
    # Create useful indicators
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

This chunk pulls 2022 ACS census tract data for Philadelphia with geometry, renames the raw variable codes to readable names, and derives two new percentage variables (transit commute share and percent white) before reprojecting to WGS84 for spatial joining with the bike trip coordinates.


Code
# Get Philadelphia census tracts
philly_census1 <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    "B01003_001",  # Total population
    "B19013_001",  # Median household income
    "B08301_001",  # Total commuters
    "B08301_010",  # Commute by transit
    "B02001_002",  # White alone
    "B25077_001"   # Median home value
  ),
  state = "PA",
  county = "Philadelphia",
  year = 2022,
  geometry = TRUE,
  output = "wide"
) %>%
  rename(
    Total_Pop1 = B01003_001E,
    Med_Inc1 = B19013_001E,
    Total_Commuters1 = B08301_001E,
    Transit_Commuters1 = B08301_010E,
    White_Pop1 = B02001_002E,
    Med_Home_Value1 = B25077_001E
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Taking_Transit1 = (Transit_Commuters1 / Total_Commuters1) * 100,
    Percent_White1 = (White_Pop1 / Total_Pop1) * 100
  ) %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)  #transforming to WGS84 for lat/lon matching, currently in NAD83


This chunk maps Philadelphia census tracts shaded by median household income, with Indego bike share trip origin points overlaid in red to visually assess whether ridership concentrates in higher- or lower-income neighborhoods.


Code
# Map median income
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Inc1), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Context for understanding bike share demand patterns"
  ) +
  # Stations 
  geom_point(
    data = indego1,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 0.25, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  mapTheme1









This chunk spatially joins each Indego station to its Philadelphia census tract to attach demographic variables, merges those demographics back onto the full trip dataset, then maps all stations flagged by whether they successfully matched to census data, highlighting unmatched stations in red to diagnose coverage gaps in non-residential tracts.


Code
# Create sf object for stations
stations_sf1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join to get census tract for each station
stations_census1 <- st_join(stations_sf1, philly_census1, left = TRUE) %>%
  st_drop_geometry()

# Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts.
stations_for_map1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc1))

# Add back to trip data
indego_census1 <- indego1 %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% 
      dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, 
             Percent_White1, Total_Pop1),
    by = "start_station"
  )


# Prepare data for visualization
stations_for_map1 <- indego1 %>%
  distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1),
    by = "start_station"
  ) %>%
  mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc1))

# Create the map showing problem stations
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = philly_census1, aes(fill = Med_Inc1), color = "white", size = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option = "viridis",
    name = "Median\nIncome",
    labels = scales::dollar,
    na.value = "grey90"
  ) +
  # Stations with census data (small grey dots)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map1 %>% filter(has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6
  ) +
  # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot)
  geom_point(
    data = stations_for_map1 %>% filter(!has_census),
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)",
    caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts"
  ) +
  mapTheme1









This chunk filters the trip data to only stations that successfully matched census tracts, then joins the demographic variables onto those trips to produce a clean dataset ready for analysis.


Code
# Identify which stations to keep
valid_stations1 <- stations_census1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(Med_Inc1)) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Filter trip data to valid stations only
indego_census1 <- indego1 %>%
  filter(start_station %in% valid_stations1) %>%
  left_join(
    stations_census1 %>% 
      dplyr::select(start_station, Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, 
             Percent_White1, Total_Pop1),
    by = "start_station"
  )


This chunk pulls hourly weather data from Philadelphia International Airport for Q2 2025, cleans and standardizes the temperature, precipitation, and wind speed fields, fills any missing hours by carrying forward the last known value, then prints a summary table of the three weather variables.


Code
# Get weather from Philadelphia International Airport (KPHL)
# This covers Q2 2025: Apr 01 - June 31
weather_data1 <- riem_measures(
  station = "PHL",  # Philadelphia International Airport
  date_start = "2025-04-01",
  date_end = "2025-06-30"
)

# Process weather data
weather_processed1 <- weather_data1 %>%
  mutate(
    interval601 = floor_date(valid, unit = "hour"),
    Temperature1 = tmpf,  # Temperature in Fahrenheit
    Precipitation1 = ifelse(is.na(p01i), 0, p01i),  # Hourly precip in inches
    Wind_Speed1 = sknt  # Wind speed in knots
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(interval601, Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1) %>%
  distinct(interval601, .keep_all = TRUE)

# Check for missing hours and interpolate if needed
weather_complete1 <- weather_processed1 %>%
  complete(interval601 = seq(min(interval601), max(interval601), by = "hour")) %>%
  fill(Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1, .direction = "down")

# Look at the weather
summary(weather_complete1 %>% dplyr::select(Temperature1, Precipitation1, Wind_Speed1))%>%
  kable()

ggplot(weather_complete1, aes(x = interval601, y = Temperature1)) +

This chunk aggregates trip data into a station-hour panel by counting departures for each station and hour combination while retaining demographic variables, then prints basic dimension checks and a histogram to inspect the distribution of hourly trip counts per station.


Code
# Count trips by station-hour
trips_panel1 <- indego_census1 %>%
  group_by(interval601, start_station, start_lat, start_lon,
           Med_Inc1, Percent_Taking_Transit1, Percent_White1, Total_Pop1) %>%
  summarize(Trip_Count1 = n()) %>%
  ungroup()

# How many station-hour observations?
nrow(trips_panel1)

hist(trips_panel1$Trip_Count1)









This chunk builds a complete station-hour panel by expanding all station and hour combinations, filling missing trip counts with zero, and rejoining station-level demographic attributes, ensuring every station has an observation for every hour even when no trips occurred.


Code
# Calculate expected panel size
n_stations1 <- length(unique(trips_panel1$start_station))
n_hours1 <- length(unique(trips_panel1$interval601))
expected_rows1 <- n_stations1 * n_hours1

cat("Expected panel rows:", format(expected_rows1, big.mark = ","), "\n")


Expected panel rows: 548,604 


Code
cat("Current rows:", format(nrow(trips_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Current rows: 179,107 


Code
cat("Missing rows:", format(expected_rows1 - nrow(trips_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Missing rows: 369,497 


Code
# Create complete panel
study_panel1 <- expand_grid(
  interval601 = unique(trips_panel1$interval601),
  start_station = unique(trips_panel1$start_station)
) %>%
  # Join trip counts
  left_join(trips_panel1 %>% 
              dplyr::select(interval601, start_station, Trip_Count1),
            by = c("interval601", "start_station")) %>%
  # Replace NA trip counts with 0
  mutate(Trip_Count1 = replace_na(Trip_Count1, 0))

# histogram of analysis
hist(study_panel1$Trip_Count1)









Code
# Fill in station attributes (they're the same for all hours)
station_attributes1 <- trips_panel1 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  summarize(
    start_lat = first(start_lat),
    start_lon = first(start_lon),
    Med_Inc1 = first(Med_Inc1),
    Percent_Taking_Transit1 = first(Percent_Taking_Transit1),
    Percent_White1 = first(Percent_White1),
    Total_Pop1 = first(Total_Pop1)
  )


study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  left_join(station_attributes1, by = "start_station")


# Verify we have complete panel
cat("Complete panel rows:", format(nrow(study_panel1), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Complete panel rows: 548,604 


This chunk extracts week, month, day of week, hour, and date from the timestamp, then flags each observation as weekend or rush hour to create time-based features for demand prediction.


Code
# Engineer time-based features from the interval timestamp for use as model predictors
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  mutate(
    week = week(interval601),
    month = month(interval601, label = TRUE),
    dotw = wday(interval601, label = TRUE),
    hour = hour(interval601),
    date = as.Date(interval601),
    weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0),
    rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0)
  )

This chunk creates lagged trip count features at 1, 2, 3, 12, and 24 hour intervals within each station, then drops the initial rows where lags cannot be computed to produce a complete dataset ready for modeling.


Code
# Sort by station and time
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  arrange(start_station, interval601)

# Create lag variables WITHIN each station
study_panel1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  group_by(start_station) %>%
  mutate(
    lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count1, 1),
    lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 2),
    lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 3),
    lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count1, 12),
    lag1day = lag(Trip_Count1, 24)
  ) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station)
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(lag1day))

cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete1), big.mark = ","), "\n")


Rows after removing NA lags: 542,556 


This chunk plots one week of trip counts at a single station alongside its 1-hour and 24-hour lagged values to visually confirm that past demand tracks closely with current demand, validating the lag features as useful predictors.


Code
# Sample one station to visualize
example_station1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>%
  head(168)  # One week

# Plot actual vs lagged demand
ggplot(example_station1, aes(x = interval601)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count1, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Current" = "red",
    "1 Hour Ago" = "lightblue",
    "24 Hours Ago" = "yellow"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station",
    subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand",

This chunk splits the panel into train and test sets by week, retaining only stations with trips in both periods to prevent data leakage, then prints observation counts and date ranges to confirm the split.


Code
# Split by week
# Q2 has weeks 14-26 (Apr - Jun)
# Train on weeks 14-22(Apr 1 - early June)
# Test on weeks 23-26 (rest of June)

# Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods?
early_stations1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week < 23) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count1 > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

late_stations1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week >= 23) %>%
  filter(Trip_Count1 > 0) %>%
  distinct(start_station) %>%
  pull(start_station)

# Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods
common_stations1 <- intersect(early_stations1, late_stations1)


# Filter panel to only common stations
study_panel_complete1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(start_station %in% common_stations1)

# NOW create train/test split
train1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week < 23)

test1 <- study_panel_complete1 %>%
  filter(week >= 23)

cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train1), big.mark = ","), "\n")

This chunk extends model 4 by adding rush hour and month fixed effects plus a rush hour and weekend interaction term, capturing the intuition that morning and evening peaks behave differently on weekdays versus weekends.


Code
# Add rush hour, month, and a rush hour-weekend interaction term
model51 <- lm(
  Trip_Count1 ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature1 + Precipitation1 +
    lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + rush_hour + as.factor(month) +
    Med_Inc1 + Percent_Taking_Transit1 + Percent_White1 +
    as.factor(start_station) +
    rush_hour * weekend,  # Rush hour effects different on weekends
  data = train1
)

This chunk generates test set predictions from all five models, computes MAE for each, and displays the results as both a formatted table and a bar chart to directly compare how much each added feature reduced prediction error.


Code
# Get predictions on test set

# Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun")))

# Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables)
contrasts(test1$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7)

test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    pred11 = predict(model11, newdata = test1),
    pred21 = predict(model21, newdata = test1),
    pred31 = predict(model31, newdata = test1),
    pred41 = predict(model41, newdata = test1),
    pred51 = predict(model51, newdata = test1)
  )

# Calculate MAE for each model
mae_results1 <- data.frame(
  Model = c(
    "1. Time + Weather",
    "2. + Temporal Lags",
    "3. + Demographics",
    "4. + Station FE",
    "5. + Rush Hour Interaction"
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred11), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred21), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred31), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(test1$Trip_Count1 - test1$pred51), na.rm = TRUE)
  )
)

kable(mae_results1, 
      digits = 3,
      caption = "Mean Absolute Error by Model (Test Set)",
      col.names = c("Model", "MAE (trips)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

This chunk computes residuals and labels each observation by time of day, then plots observed versus predicted trip counts in a faceted scatter plot split by time period and weekend status to diagnose where model 4 over- or underpredicts.


Code
# Calculate errors and label each observation by time of day
test1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(
    error = Trip_Count1 - pred41,
    abs_error = abs(error),
    time_of_day = case_when(
      hour < 7 ~ "Overnight",
      hour >= 7 & hour < 10 ~ "AM Rush",
      hour >= 10 & hour < 15 ~ "Mid-Day",
      hour >= 15 & hour <= 18 ~ "PM Rush",
      hour > 18 ~ "Evening"
    )
  )

# Scatter plot by time and day type
ggplot(test1, aes(x = Trip_Count1, y = pred41)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, color = "#3182bd") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkgreen") +
  facet_grid(weekend ~ time_of_day) +
  labs(
    title = "Observed vs. Predicted Bike Trips",
    subtitle = "Model 4 performance by time period",
    x = "Observed Trips",
    y = "Predicted Trips",
    caption = "Red line = perfect predictions; Green line = actual model fit"
  ) +
  plotTheme1

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
Comparison with Q1 Data
How do MAE values compare? Why might they differ?
MAE for Q1 below:

Compared to Q2, the MAE for Q1 is consistently lower across all models, suggesting that Q1 data is more predictable. Q2 shows greater demand variability because overall ridership increases in the second quarter compared to the first. With increased ridership, patterns change, making it more difficult to predict demand accurately.
Are temporal patterns different (e.g., summer vs. winter?
Temporal patterns are definitely different in the spring vs the winter. First, the spring has higher usage than the winter. Another, from early days of Q1 to late Q1 the ridership kept increasing, in a steeper slope. However, for this one the ridership increases slower with a more flattened direction.
Which features are most important in your quarter?
The most important feature is the temporal lag, where the MAE dropped from 0.835 to 0.690, a 0.145 point decrease, the most substantive change across all models. Therefore, temporal lag is the most important feature in this case.

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
1. Create error maps
We pick Model 4, with the lowest MAE for the error analysis. This contains all features included other than the rush hour effect. The rush hour increased our MAE therefore, Model 4 was chosen. The error map below showcases where the errors are, and attempts to explain why.


Code
#station error 
station_errors1 <- test1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred41)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    MAE       = mean(abs(Trip_Count1 - pred41), na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_trips = mean(Trip_Count1, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_obs     = n(),
    .groups   = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

#Convert stations to spatial feature
station_errors_sf1 <- station_errors1 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatial join-  each station to its census tract
station_errors_tract1 <- st_join(
  station_errors_sf1,
  philly_census1 %>% select(GEOID, geometry),   # philly_census already in 4326
  join = st_within
)

#aggregate it to tract level
tract_errors1 <- station_errors_tract1 %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarize(
    tract_MAE1      = weighted.mean(MAE, w = n_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
    tract_demand1   = mean(avg_trips, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_stations1     = n(),
    .groups        = "drop"
  )

#join to tract geometry
philly_tract_errors1 <- philly_census1 %>%
  left_join(tract_errors1, by = "GEOID")

#Map 1 tract level error
ahm1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data  = philly_tract_errors1,
    aes(fill = tract_MAE1),
    color = "white", linewidth = 0.02
  ) +
  scale_fill_viridis(
    option   = "plasma",
    name     = "Weighted\nMAE (trips)",
    direction = 1,
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  # Overlay station dots for reference
  geom_point(
    data  = station_errors,
    aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat),
    color = "white", size = 0.008, alpha = 0.05
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Prediction Error by Census Tract",
    subtitle = "Model 4, weighted mean MAE per tract (test set, weeks 23–26)",
    caption  = "Grey tracts have no Indego stations"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


#mapping neighborhoods by residual errors
res1 <- test1 %>%
  mutate(residual = Trip_Count1 - pred41)
  
# Calculate mean residual per station
station_residuals1 <- res1 %>%
  filter(!is.na(pred41)) %>%
  group_by(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_residual = mean(residual, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_obs         = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon))

# Convert stations to spatial object
station_residuals_sf1 <- station_residuals1 %>%
  st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326)

# Spatially join stations to census tracts
station_residuals_tract1 <- st_join(
  station_residuals_sf1,
  philly_census1 %>% dplyr::select(GEOID, geometry),
  join = st_within
)

# Aggregate residuals to tract level
tract_residuals1 <- station_residuals_tract1 %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(GEOID) %>%
  summarize(
    tract_mean_residual = weighted.mean(mean_residual, w = n_obs, na.rm = TRUE),
    n_stations          = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Join residuals back to tract geometry for mapping
philly_tract_residuals1 <- philly_census1 %>%
  left_join(tract_residuals1, by = "GEOID")


#mapping it
ahm2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data  = philly_tract_residuals1,
    aes(fill = tract_mean_residual),
    color = "white", linewidth = 0.02
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low      = "darkred",     # red  = overpredicted (model too high)
    high     = "darkblue",  # blue = underpredicted (model too low)
    midpoint = 0,
    name     = "Mean\nResidual\n(trips)",
    na.value = "grey85"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Where Does Our Model Struggle?",
    subtitle = "Mean Signed Residual by Census Tract (test set, weeks 23–26)",
    caption  = "Blue = model underestimates demand | Red = model overestimates demand\nGrey tracts have no Indego stations"
  ) +
  mapTheme1


(ahm1)











Code
# map of errors
(ahm2)









Identify neighborhoods with high errors
As we can observe in the map, the errors are mostly concentrated around the Center City area and periphery. , which is also where the model seemingly under-predicts.
Hypothesize why (missing features? different demand patterns?)
This happens primarily because of high demand fluctuation in this region. There may be unobserved factors contributing to this, or other missing spatial features that need to be considered in our model. Center City, as the name suggests, consists of the urban core of the Philadelphia region, leading to travel patterns driven by a wide range of trip purposes. As a result, it is not always certain what the demand pattern in this region is going to be.

2. Temporal Error
When are errors highest?

Tuesday PM rush hour, around 17:00 or 5:00 PM, seems to have the greatest MAE.

Do certain hours/days have systematic under/over-prediction?
Monday and Tuesday during evening rush hour at 5 PM, the model tends to underpredict demand. Similarly, Wednesday at 8 AM shows the highest underprediction, and Monday at 8 AM also exhibits underprediction. Interestingly, on Friday nights at 11 PM, the model also underpredicts demand.
Conversely, the model overpredicts on Saturday and Sunday mornings, as well as during the 5 PM rush hour on those days.
Are there seasonal patterns?
For quarter 1 the patterns are as such presented below:



Comparing it with the one for the second quarter, the spatial patterns are both similar and different. For underprediction, Tuesday and Monday remain constant in the top five. However, interestingly, Saturday at 1 PM is also underpredicted. This may perhaps be attributed to winter weekend afternoons being warmer and more favorable. Thursday at 5 PM is also underpredicted in the first quarter, which is not observed in the second-quarter data.
For overprediction, Saturday at 8 AM and Saturday at 5 PM remain constant. However, Thursday at 8 PM seems to be overpredicted in Quarter 1, which is absent from Quarter 2.

Relate errors to census characteristics - Are certain communities systematically harder to predict- What are the equity implications?
Among the considered census characteristics, the ones that seem to visually coincide with the errors are median home value and, consequently, median household income as well. This gives us some direction in understanding how unpredictable the demand patterns are in these considerably high-income areas, which coincide with Center City, Philadelphia’s central business district. Errors are likely to be concentrated here because these areas may be sensitive to many spatial, economic, and temporal factors.

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
Operational Implications
Compared to the the rest of the models the MAE of 0.661 of the poisson distribution is good enough for Compared to the rest of the models, the MAE of 0.661 for the Poisson distribution is good enough for implementation in this case. However, when considering the data from Quarter 1, which had a lower MAE in the range of 0.5 to 0.6, this performance is not good enough. In our case, the model seems to overpredict more than it underpredicts, and this may create problems for rebalancing, as more bikes than demanded may be circulated unnecessarily, causing financial stress for Indego. I would recommend implementing this system under strict precautions to avoid an oversupply of cycles.

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
Equity Considerations
The prediction errors, as earlier mentioned, disproportionately affect central areas. However, as evidenced in the mean residual of the Poisson model above, the error is centered around Center City, with some areas in the western part of Philadelphia also affected. University City is also affected by overprediction; however, this is not an equity concern, but rather a managerial one. There is minimal underprediction. While there are some areas

title: Lab 5: Bikeshare SpaceTime Prediction
text:
Model Limitations
The model has several important limitations that must be acknowledged. First, the weather data comes from a single airport station, and thus may not accurately capture the hyperlocal weather conditions across Philadelphia’s many different neighborhoods. Beyond this, there are many unobserved demand drivers, such as events, construction activity, or street-level urban conditions, that are completely absent from the model. This becomes more evident given that the spatial features added in Part 3, namely transit proximity, commercial corridors, and holidays, did not contribute much to improving predictive performance, perhaps suggesting that other important factors still remain uncaptured.
Further, the model relies heavily on station fixed effects, which limits its ability to generalize to new or hypothetical stations. Structurally as well, the fixed-coefficient framework appears to struggle with the more complex and volatile demand patterns of dense urban cores such as Center City, where the errors seem to be more concentrated. In addition, the lag structure adds another layer of limitation, because an error in one hour can carry forward and affect the lagged predictions in the following hours. Finally, the demographic analysis is conducted at the census tract level, which may hide more fine-grained inequities at the block or neighborhood scale or vs using fishnet, and thus may understate disparities in model performance for certain communities.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
You are a data analyst for the California Department of Human Services. The department is considering implementing an algorithmic system to identify communities that should receive priority for social service funding and outreach programs. Your supervisor has asked you to evaluate the quality and reliability of available census data to inform this decision.
Drawing on our Week 2 discussion of algorithmic bias, you need to assess not just what the data shows, but how reliable it is and what communities might be affected by data quality issues.




Apply dplyr functions to real census data for policy analysis
Evaluate data quality using margins of error
Connect technical analysis to algorithmic decision-making
Identify potential equity implications of data reliability issues
Create professional documentation for policy stakeholders




Submit by posting your updated portfolio link on Canvas. Your assignment should be accessible at your-portfolio-url/labs/lab_1/
Make sure to update your _quarto.yml navigation to include this assignment under an “Labs” menu.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
You are a data analyst for the California Department of Human Services. The department is considering implementing an algorithmic system to identify communities that should receive priority for social service funding and outreach programs. Your supervisor has asked you to evaluate the quality and reliability of available census data to inform this decision.
Drawing on our Week 2 discussion of algorithmic bias, you need to assess not just what the data shows, but how reliable it is and what communities might be affected by data quality issues.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
Apply dplyr functions to real census data for policy analysis
Evaluate data quality using margins of error
Connect technical analysis to algorithmic decision-making
Identify potential equity implications of data reliability issues
Create professional documentation for policy stakeholders

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
Submit by posting your updated portfolio link on Canvas. Your assignment should be accessible at your-portfolio-url/labs/lab_1/
Make sure to update your _quarto.yml navigation to include this assignment under an “Labs” menu.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
2.1 Data Retrieval
Your Task: Use get_acs() to retrieve county-level data for your chosen state.
Requirements: - Geography: county level - Variables: median household income (B19013_001) and total population (B01003_001)
- Year: 2022 - Survey: acs5 - Output format: wide
Hint: Remember to give your variables descriptive names using the variables = c(name = "code") syntax.

# Write your get_acs() code here
ca_county_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "county",
  state = my_state,
  variables = c(
    median_hh_income = "B19013_001",
    total_pop = "B01003_001"
  ),
  year= 2022,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "wide"
)

head(ca_county_data)%>%
  kable()

# Clean the county names to remove state name and "County" 
# Hint: use mutate() with str_remove()

clean_data <- ca_county_data %>%
  mutate(
    NAME = NAME %>%
      str_remove(", California") %>%
      str_remove(" County")
  )%>%
  select(`County` = "NAME",
         `Median Household Income` = "median_hh_incomeE",
         `Median HH Income Margin of Error` = "median_hh_incomeM",
        `Total Population` = "total_popE",
        )

# Display the first few rows
head(clean_data)%>%
  kable()

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
2.2 Data Quality Assessment
Your Task: Calculate margin of error percentages and create reliability categories.
Requirements: - Calculate MOE percentage: (margin of error / estimate) * 100 - Create reliability categories: - High Confidence: MOE < 5% - Moderate Confidence: MOE 5-10%
- Low Confidence: MOE > 10% - Create a flag for unreliable estimates (MOE > 10%)
Hint: Use mutate() with case_when() for the categories.

# Calculate MOE percentage and reliability categories using mutate()
ca_county_reliability <- clean_data %>%
  mutate(
    moe_pct = (`Median HH Income Margin of Error`/`Median Household Income`)*100,
    category = case_when(
      moe_pct < 5 ~ "High Confidence",
      moe_pct >= 5 & moe_pct <= 10 ~ "Moderate Confidence",
      moe_pct > 10 ~ "Low Confidence"
      ),
    flag = case_when(category == "Low Confidence" ~ "!!!",
                   TRUE ~ "    "  )
)
  

tibble(ca_county_reliability)%>%
  kable()

# Hint: use count() and mutate() to add percentages

count_reliability <- ca_county_reliability %>%
  count(category) %>%
  mutate(
    percent = n/sum(n)*100
  )%>%
  select(`Category of Reliability` ="category",
         `Count`= "n", 
         `Percentage`= "percent")

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
2.3 High Uncertainty Counties
Your Task: Identify the 5 counties with the highest MOE percentages.
Requirements: - Sort by MOE percentage (highest first) - Select the top 5 counties - Display: county name, median income, margin of error, MOE percentage, reliability category - Format as a professional table using kable()
Hint: Use arrange(), slice(), and select() functions.

# Create table of top 5 counties by MOE percentage

top_variable <- ca_county_reliability %>%
  arrange(desc(moe_pct))%>%
  slice(1:5) 
  
head(top_variable)%>%
  kable()

# Format as table with kable() - include appropriate column names and caption
top_variable <- ca_county_reliability %>%
  arrange(desc(moe_pct)) %>%
  slice(1:5)

top_variable %>%
  select(
    County,
    `Median Household Income`,
    `Median HH Income Margin of Error`,
    `Total Population`,
    `Margin of Error for HH Income (%)` = moe_pct,
    `Reliability Category` = category,
    DANGER = flag
  ) %>%
  kable(caption = "Top 5 counties in California by HH Income MOE")

Data Quality Commentary: These results show that counties with the highest margins of error for median household income, such as Mono, Alpine, Sierra, Trinity, and Plumas, have less reliable income estimates. Algorithms that rely on this data may therefore poorly serve residents in these counties by producing unstable classifications or inaccurate eligibility decisions. This uncertainty may lead to their exclusion or misrepresentation in planning decisions and program targeting that depend on precise income thresholds.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
3.1 Focus Area Selection
Your Task: Select 2-3 counties from your reliability analysis for detailed tract-level study.
Strategy: Choose counties that represent different reliability levels (e.g., 1 high confidence, 1 moderate, 1 low confidence) to compare how data quality varies.

# Use filter() to select 2-3 counties from your county_reliability data
# Store the selected counties in a variable called selected_counties
# Display the selected counties with their key characteristics
# Show: county name, median income, MOE percentage, reliability category


selected_counties <- ca_county_reliability %>%
  filter(category %in% c("Low Confidence", "Moderate Confidence", "High Confidence")) %>%
  group_by(category) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(
    County,
    `Median Household Income`,
    `Margin of Error for HH Income (%)` = moe_pct,
    `Reliability Category` = category,
  )

selected_counties %>% 
  kable()










County
Median Household Income
Margin of Error for HH Income (%)
Reliability Category

Comment on the output: The % cateogory of marginal error directly reflects the reliability of the data. Where, lower the % of marginal error, higher reliability of data.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
3.2 Tract-Level Demographics
Your Task: Get demographic data for census tracts in your selected counties.
Requirements: - Geography: tract level - Variables: white alone (B03002_003), Black/African American (B03002_004), Hispanic/Latino (B03002_012), total population (B03002_001) - Use the same state and year as before - Output format: wide - Challenge: You’ll need county codes, not names. Look at the GEOID patterns in your county data for hints.

# Define your race/ethnicity variables with descriptive names
race_variables <- c(
  `White Alone` = "B03002_003",
  `Black/African American` = "B03002_004",
  `Hispanic/Latino` = "B03002_012",
  `Total Population` = "B03002_001"
)

# Use get_acs() to retrieve tract-level data
# Hint: You may need to specify county codes in the county parameter

county_levelcode <- c("001","003","005")
  

selected_counties_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = race_variables,
  county = county_levelcode,
  state = my_state,
  year = 2022,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "wide"
)

tibble(selected_counties_data)%>%
  kable()

# Calculate percentage of each group using mutate()

percentage_eachgroup <- selected_counties_data %>%
  mutate(
    pct_white = (`White AloneE`/`Total PopulationE`)*100,
    pct_Black = (`Black/African AmericanE`/`Total PopulationE`)*100,
    pct_Hisp = (`Hispanic/LatinoE`/`Total PopulationE`)*100
    ) %>%
  # Create percentages for white, Black, and Hispanic populations
  mutate(`Percentage of White, Black and Hispanic Populations`= pct_white + pct_Black + pct_Hisp, 
 # Add readable tract and county name columns using str_extract() or similar
         Tract =  str_extract(NAME, "[0-9.]+"),
         County = str_extract(NAME, "[A-Za-z ]+ County"))%>% 
  select(County, Tract,
         `White %` = "pct_white",
         `Black %`= "pct_Black",
         `Hispanic or Latino %`="pct_Hisp", 
         `Percentage of White, Black and Hispanic Populations`,
        )
tibble(percentage_eachgroup)%>%
  kable()

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
3.3 Demographic Analysis
Your Task: Analyze the demographic patterns in your selected areas.

# Find the tract with the highest percentage of Hispanic/Latino residents
# Hint: use arrange() and slice() to get the top tract
hisp_high <- percentage_eachgroup %>%
  arrange(desc(`Hispanic or Latino %`))%>%
  slice_head(n=1)%>%
  select(County,`Hispanic or Latino %`)
tibble(hisp_high)%>%
  kable()




County
Hispanic or Latino %




Alameda County
85




# Calculate average demographics by county using group_by() and summarize()
# Show: number of tracts, average percentage for each racial/ethnic group
county_summary <- percentage_eachgroup %>%
  group_by(County)%>%
  summarise(`Number of Tracts`=n(),
            `Average White Percentage` = mean(`White %`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Average Black Percentage` = mean(`Black %`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Average Hispanic Percentage` = mean(`Hispanic or Latino %`, na.rm = TRUE)
    
  )%>%
  select(
    County,
    `Number of Tracts`,
    `Average White Percentage`,
    `Average Black Percentage`,
    `Average Hispanic Percentage`
    )
tibble(county_summary)%>% 
  kable()

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
4.1 MOE Analysis for Demographic Variables
Your Task: Examine margins of error for demographic variables to see if some communities have less reliable data.
Requirements: - Calculate MOE percentages for each demographic variable - Flag tracts where any demographic variable has MOE > 15% - Create summary statistics

# Calculate MOE percentages for white, Black, and Hispanic variables
# Hint: use the same formula as before (margin/estimate * 100)
moe_race <- selected_counties_data %>%
  mutate(
    `MOE % White` = (`White AloneM` / `White AloneE`)*100,
    `MOE % Black` = (`Black/African AmericanM` / `Black/African AmericanE`)*100,
    `MOE % Hispanic` = (`Hispanic/LatinoM` / `Hispanic/LatinoE`)*100,

# Create a flag for tracts with high MOE on any demographic variable
# Use logical operators (| for OR) in an ifelse() statement
Flag = ifelse(
     `MOE % White` > 15 | `MOE % Black` > 15 | `MOE % Hispanic` > 15, "UNRELIABLE", "  ")
    )
tibble(moe_race)%>%
  kable()

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
4.2 Pattern Analysis
Your Task: Investigate whether data quality problems are randomly distributed or concentrated in certain types of communities.

# Group tracts by whether they have high MOE issues
# Calculate average characteristics for each group:
# - population size, demographic percentages
# Use group_by() and summarize() to create this comparison
# Create a professional table showing the patterns

moe_issuesummary <- moe_race %>%
  group_by(Flag) %>%
  summarise(
    tracts = n(),
    `Average Population` = mean(`Total PopulationE`, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Average White Population %` = mean((`White AloneE` / `Total PopulationE`) * 100, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Average Black Population %` = mean((`Black/African AmericanE` / `Total PopulationE`) * 100, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Average Hispanic Population %` = mean((`Hispanic/LatinoE` / `Total PopulationE`) * 100, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

  tibble(moe_issuesummary) %>%
    kable(
    caption = "Average Tract Characteristics by Data Quality Status"
  )

Pattern Analysis: In my selected counties, all 390 census tracts are flagged as unreliable, meaning there is no meaningful contrast between reliable and unreliable areas. These tracts are relatively small, with an average population of about 4,374 residents, which helps explain the uniformly high margins of error. While White, Black, and Hispanic populations are present at non-negligible levels across tracts, the high uncertainty affects all groups similarly, indicating that unreliability is driven primarily by small population sizes and ACS sampling limitations rather than demographic composition. As a result, tract-level estimates in these counties are particularly vulnerable to error, making them risky inputs for algorithmic decision-making that depends on precise demographic thresholds.

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
5.1 Analysis Integration and Professional Summary
Your Task: Write an executive summary that integrates findings from all four analyses.
Executive Summary Requirements: 1. Overall Pattern Identification: What are the systematic patterns across all your analyses? 2. Equity Assessment: Which communities face the greatest risk of algorithmic bias based on your findings? 3. Root Cause Analysis: What underlying factors drive both data quality issues and bias risk? 4. Strategic Recommendations: What should the Department implement to address these systematic issues?
Executive Summary:
The overall analysis focused on the reliability of ACS data for median household income at the county level and racial and ethnic composition at the census-tract level shows a clear understanding of how data reliability varies across measures. Data reliability differs by both variable and geographic or political division. County-level income estimates are mostly reliable, with almost 70 percent of counties providing data where the margin of error falls below 5 percent. In contrast, census-tract-level racial composition data are largely unreliable. For the randomly selected counties, regardless of income data reliability, racial data reliability remained consistently low. Out of 390 census tracts analyzed, all exhibited unreliable racial or ethnic estimates failing the 15% threshold.
Data reliability is especially weak for race and ethnicity variables, primarily because margins of error are high across tracts. This suggests a need to reconsider current data collection approaches. Census tracts in the analysis have relatively small populations, averaging around 4,000 residents. A general inference from the results is that smaller population sizes are associated with higher margins of error. As a result, existing ACS sampling and data collection methods may require revision to produce more reliable tract-level estimates.
The root cause of these issues appears to lie in the sampling design and overall data collection methodology. Capturing a larger effective sample would be necessary to improve reliability. In the long term, reconsidering census tract sizing or adopting alternative aggregation strategies may be necessary to support more stable and reliable data collection in the future.
The Department of Human Services should adopt an uncertainty-aware approach to decision-making by incorporating margins of error directly into analytical and algorithmic processes rather than relying on point estimates alone. Census tract level racial and ethnic composition data should not be used in isolation under current reliability conditions and should instead be aggregated to higher geographies or supplemented with additional data. A tiered framework based on data reliability should guide when automated decisions are appropriate versus when further review is required. Finally, reliability flags and margin of error measures should be clearly documented and carried through all outputs so uncertainty is visible at the point of decision

title: Lab 1: Census Data Quality for Policy Decisions
text:
6.3 Specific Recommendations
Your Task: Create a decision framework for algorithm implementation.

# Create a summary table using your county reliability data
# Include: county name, median income, MOE percentage, reliability category

# Add a new column with algorithm recommendations using case_when():
# - High Confidence: "Safe for algorithmic decisions"
# - Moderate Confidence: "Use with caution - monitor outcomes"  
# - Low Confidence: "Requires manual review or additional data"

# Format as a professional table with kable()

summary_overall <- ca_county_reliability %>%
  select(
    County,
    `Median Household Income`,
    `Margin of Error for HH Income (%)` = moe_pct, 
    `Reliability Category` = category
  )%>%
  mutate(
    `Algorithm Recommendation` = case_when(
    `Reliability Category` == "High Confidence" ~ "Safe for Algorithmic Decisions",
    `Reliability Category` == "Moderate Confidence" ~ "Use with caution - monitor outcomes",
    `Reliability Category` == "Low Confidence" ~ "Requires manual review or additional data"
    )
  )
tibble(summary_overall)%>%
  kable(caption = "County Income Data Reliability and Guide for Data Use - Algorithmic Recommendations <br> <em>For ethnic and race related analysis manual analysis and further data cleaning required")

title: Lab 2: Spatial Analysis and Visualization
section: Part 1: Healthcare Access for Vulnerable Populations
text:
Part 1: Healthcare Access for Vulnerable Populations

Research Question
Which Pennsylvania counties have the highest proportion of vulnerable populations (elderly + low-income) living far from hospitals?


Analysis Steps

Step 1: Data Collection

# Load required packages - loaded all packages I could 
library(tidyverse)
library(tidycensus)
library(scales)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(sf)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(tidycensus)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)
library(knitr)

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)
options(tigris_progress = FALSE)

# Load spatial data
pa_counties <- st_read("data/Pennsylvania_County_Boundaries.shp")
hospitals <- st_read("data/hospitals.geojson")
census_tracts <- tracts(state = "PA", cb = TRUE)


#Transformed to Same County
hospitals <- st_transform(hospitals, st_crs(pa_counties))
census_tracts <- st_transform(census_tracts, st_crs(pa_counties))


# Checking that all data loaded correctly
head(pa_counties)
head(hospitals)
head(census_tracts)
head(census_tracts)


#summarizing to see what Projection is used
summarise(pa_counties)

Questions to answer:.

How many hospitals are in your dataset?.
223

How many census tracts?.
3,345

What coordinate reference system is each dataset in?.
All use WGS 84 after I transformed the coordinate system.




Step 2: Get Demographic Data

# Get demographic data from ACS
demographic_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  state = "PA",
  variables = c(
    total_population = "B01003_001",
    median_income = "B19013_001",
#used chatgpt to get tables for all male and female categries above 65
    a = "B01001_020",   
    b = "B01001_021",
    c = "B01001_022",
    d = "B01001_023",
    e = "B01001_024",
    f = "B01001_025",
    g = "B01001_044",
    h = "B01001_045",
    i = "B01001_046",
    j = "B01001_047",
    k = "B01001_048",
    l = "B01001_049"
  ),
  year = 2022,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "wide"
) %>%
#adding all categories for 65+ populations to create and aggregate whole.
  mutate(           
    pop_65plusE =
      aE + bE + cE + dE + eE + fE + gE + hE + iE + jE + kE + lE
      ) %>%
#removing excess strings (words) to make data more presentable
  mutate(
    NAME = NAME %>%
      str_remove("Census Tract ") %>%
      str_remove("; Pennsylvania")
  )%>%
#selecting what to present
  select(
    GEOID,
    `County Name` = NAME,
    `Total Population` = total_populationE,
    `Total Population 65 Y+` = pop_65plusE,
    `Median HH Income` = median_incomeE
  )


#presenting only the first six rows to check for data
head(demographic_data)
 
   
#using left_join to join demographic data to census tracts using common GEOID
join_tract_boundaries <- census_tracts %>%
  left_join(demographic_data, by = "GEOID")


#viewing the first six rows of the new joined data
head(join_tract_boundaries) 

  
#summarising only the median household income data 
join_tract_boundaries%>%
  select(`Median HH Income`)%>%
  summary()%>%
  kable(digit = 1)

Questions to answer:.

What year of ACS data are you using?.



How many tracts have missing income data?.



What is the median income across all PA census tracts?.
70,188.




Step 3: Define Vulnerable Populations

# Filtering for vulnerable tracts based criteria (less than Q3 median incme and more than 20% those above 65 years)
identify_vulnerability <- join_tract_boundaries %>%
  mutate(
    plus_pct = (`Total Population 65 Y+` / `Total Population`) * 100,
    category = case_when(
      `Median HH Income` < 55924 & plus_pct > 20 ~ "Vulnerable",
      TRUE ~ "Not Vulnerable"
    )
  )


#counts number of vulnerable cases per category, and percentage of vulnerable vs non vulnerable
count(identify_vulnerability, category)%>%
  mutate(
    percentage_vulner = (n / sum(n))*100 
#n is the number per category #sum(n) is total count
  )%>%
  select(
    category,
    n, 
    percentage_vulner)%>%
  kable(digit = 1)


#viewing the data for vulnerability that has been identified
head(identify_vulnerability)%>%
  kable(digit = 1)

Questions to answer:.

What income threshold did you choose and why?.
less than 55,943 (Lower Quartile)
What elderly population threshold did you choose and why?.
I chose elderly population over 65 year old, and in areas where the
population is more than 20%.
How many tracts meet your vulnerability criteria?.



What percentage of PA census tracts are considered vulnerable by your definition?.
7.9%.




Step 4: Calculate Distance to Hospitals

# Transform to appropriate projected CRS, which is 3365, NAD38 PA south. Best for distance calculations
hos <- st_transform(hospitals, 3365)
vul <- st_transform(identify_vulnerability, 3365)


# Calculate distance from each tract centroid to nearest hospital
vul_new <- vul %>%
  filter(category == "Vulnerable") %>% 
  mutate(
    dist_near_hos = as.numeric(
      st_distance(
      st_centroid(.), st_union(hos) #st_union makes scattered hospital data into one unit that can be used for calculation 
    )
    ) / 1609.34 #converting metres to miles 
    )


#viewing distance calculated from centroid of vulnerable tract to nearest hospital
head(vul_new)%>%
  kable(digit=1)


#calculating summary of distance from hospital data
new_summary <- vul_new %>%
  select(dist_near_hos)%>%
  summary()


#viewing the calculation from above
new_summary%>%
  kable()


#identifying (counting) vulnerable tracts more than 15 miles from hospital
dist_15 <- vul_new%>%
  filter(dist_near_hos > 15 )%>%
  count()%>%
  select(n) %>%
  st_drop_geometry(.)


#viewing above calculation
dist_15%>%
  kable()

I chose my projection as 3365 because it is the apt projection for Pennsylvania South for distance calculations.
Questions to answer:.
What is the average distance to the nearest hospital for vulnerable tracts? 14.88 Miles
What is the maximum distance?.
84.06 Miles
How many vulnerable tracts are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital?.
85



Step 5: Identify Underserved Areas
“underserved” are vulnerable tracts that are more than 15 miles from the nearest hospital.

# Create underserved variable, categorizing into underserved and not
underserved <- vul_new %>%
  mutate(
    serv_under_or = case_when(
      dist_near_hos > 15 ~ "underserved",
      TRUE ~ "vulnerable but served"
    )
  )


#counting number of underserved and vulnerable but served counties, then finding the percentage within each category
new1_summary <- underserved %>%
  count(serv_under_or)%>%
  mutate (
    underserv_pct = (n/sum(n)*100)
  )%>%
  select(n, serv_under_or, underserv_pct)


#viewing what was calculated above
glimpse(new1_summary)

Rows: 2
Columns: 4
$ n             <int> 85, 188
$ serv_under_or <chr> "underserved", "vulnerable but served"
$ underserv_pct <dbl> 31.13553, 68.86447
$ geometry      <MULTIPOLYGON [US_survey_foot]> MULTIPOLYGON (((1506307 154..., MULTIPOLYGON (((1365750 …


Questions to answer: - How many tracts are underserved?.
85 are underserved - What percentage of vulnerable tracts are underserved?.
31.1% are underserved - Does this surprise you? Why or why not?.
I do not have any context for “surprise” here, however I am concerned that 31% areas are underserved. Therefore more intervention and planning is required.



Step 6: Aggregate to County Level

# transforming to NAD83 again string in different variable
pcounty <- st_transform(pa_counties, 3365)
pcensustract <- st_transform(census_tracts, 3365)


#creating spatial join of underserved(categorized) and pa counties
new_spatial_join <- underserved %>%
  st_join(pcounty) %>%
  group_by(COUNTY_NAM) %>% #creating groups by county
  summarise(              
# summarise will now place these calculations in the groups of group_by counties
    total_vulnerable = n(),
    total_underserved = sum(serv_under_or == "underserved"),
    pct_underserved = (total_underserved / total_vulnerable) * 100,
    avg_dist = mean(dist_near_hos),
    total_vul_pop = sum(`Total Population`)
  )


#ensuring compliance with projection system 
 st_transform(new_spatial_join, 3365)

title: Lab 2: Spatial Analysis and Visualization
text:
Part 2: Comprehensive Visualization
Using the skills from Week 3 (Data Visualization), create publication-quality maps and charts.

Map 1: County-Level Choropleth

# Create county-level access map
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = county_map_data, aes(fill = pct_underserved), color = "black") +
  geom_sf(data = hos, color = "green", size = 0.9, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "% Underserved",option = "inferno", na.value = "navy") +
  labs(
    title = "Underserved Vulnerable Tracts",
    subtitle = "% of vulnerable counties that are underserved",
    caption = "Green points = Hospitals"
  ) +
  theme_void()











Map 2: Detailed Vulnerability Map

# Create detailed tract-level map
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = county_map_data, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_sf(data = underserved, aes(fill = serv_under_or), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = hos, color = "green", size = 0.9, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Status", option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    title = "Underserved Vulnerable Tracts",
    subtitle = "Tracts vulnerable and more than 15 miles from a hospital are underserved",
    caption = "Green points = Hospitals"
  ) +
  theme_void()











Chart: Distribution Analysis

# Create distribution visualization
ggplot(vul_new) +
  aes(x = dist_near_hos) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "navy", color = "white", alpha = 0.7) +
   geom_vline(xintercept = 15, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Distances to Nearest Hospital",
    x = "Distance to Nearest Hospital (miles)",
    y = "Number of Vulnerable Tracts",
    subtitle = "red line represents the 15 mile threshold for underserved",
    caption = "most vulnerable census tracts have access to a hospital within 25 miles,
    with high concentration of around 90 tracts with hospital within 5-10 miles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Lab 2: Spatial Analysis and Visualization
section: Part 3: Bring Your Own Data Analysis
text:
Part 3: Bring Your Own Data Analysis

Infrastructure & Services
Polling Place Accessibility - Data: Polling Places, SEPTA stops, Census tracts (elderly population, disability rates) - Question: “Are polling places accessible for elderly and disabled voters?” - Operations: Buffer polling places and transit stops, identify vulnerable populations, find areas lacking access - Policy relevance: Voting rights, election infrastructure, ADA compliance

Find and load additional data


# Load your additional dataset
phil_censustract <- st_read("data/Census_Tracts_2010-shp (3)")
polling_places <- st_read("data/polling_places")
transit_stops <- st_read("data/Transit_Stops_(Spring_2025)")

shub <- st_transform(phil_censustract, 3365)
dhub <- st_transform(polling_places,3365)
lhub <- st_transform(transit_stops,3365)

Questions to answer: - What dataset did you choose and why?.
I chose polling places, transit stops and census tracts(for Philadelphia) to analyze voting accessibility.

What is the data source and date?.

The data here are shape files from OpenDataPhilly, all transit Stops as of
2025, census tracts as of 2010, polling places (no date available)

How many features does it contain?.
Census Tract (384), Polling Places (1,703), Transit Stops (22,478)
What CRS is it in? Did you need to transform it?.
I transformed all to 3365 (NAD83)



Pose a research question.

Do elderly populations have adequate access to polling places via public transit?


Conduct spatial analysis


# Your spatial analysis


#demographic data from census data using get_acs
a_demographic_data <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  state = "PA",
  county = "PHILADELPHIA",
  variables = c(
    atotal_population = "B01003_001",
    a = "B01001_020",                  #extracting up all 65+ categories 
    b = "B01001_021",
    c = "B01001_022",
    d = "B01001_023",
    e = "B01001_024",
    f = "B01001_025",
    g = "B01001_044",
    h = "B01001_045",
    i = "B01001_046",
    j = "B01001_047",
    k = "B01001_048",
    l = "B01001_049"
  ),
  year = 2022,
  survey = "acs5",
  output = "wide"
) %>%                                  #calculating the total population 65+
  mutate(
    apop_65plusE = aE + bE + cE + dE + eE + fE + gE + hE + iE + jE + kE + lE
  ) %>%
  mutate(                              #cleaning (removing) strings that were unnecessary
    NAME = NAME %>%
      str_remove("Census Tract ") %>%
      str_remove("; Pennsylvania")
  ) %>%
  select(                               #rename relevant columns for analysis
    GEOID,
    `County Name` = NAME,
    `Total Population` = atotal_populationE,
    `Total Population 65 Y+` = apop_65plusE
  )


# joining demographic data to census tracts in Philadelphia
a_census_tract_join <- shub %>%
  left_join(a_demographic_data, by = c("GEOID10" = "GEOID"))


# identifying vulnerable tracts 1/3 mile distance from centroid to polling places
a_vulnerable_tracts <- a_census_tract_join %>%
  mutate(
    percent = (`Total Population 65 Y+` / `Total Population`) * 100,
    a_distance = as.numeric(st_distance(st_centroid(shub), st_union(dhub))),
    vulnerability = case_when(
      percent > 40 & a_distance > 600 ~ "Vulnerable!",
      TRUE ~ "Not Vulnerable"
    )
  )



# filter to vulnerable tracts
identify_vulnerabilitya <- a_vulnerable_tracts %>%
  filter(vulnerability == "Vulnerable!")


# creating buffer of 0.125 mile around transit stops (easy access for senior citizes)
stop_buffer <- lhub %>%
  st_buffer(200)


#identifying critical tracts (using disjoint to exclude areas not covered by the transit buffer)
critical_tracts <- identify_vulnerabilitya %>%
  st_filter(stop_buffer, .predicate = st_disjoint) 


#Identifying most priritizable among the critical tracts, prioritizing based on distance to polling place, followed by percent of 65+
top_critical_tracts <- critical_tracts %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  arrange(desc(a_distance), desc(percent)) %>%
  mutate(
    `Tract` = str_remove(NAMELSAD10, "Census Tract "),
    `Total Population` = comma(`Total Population`),
    `Avg Distance to Polling Places (mi)` = a_distance / 1609.34
  ) %>%
  select(
    `Tract`,
    `Total Population`,
    `% Vulnerable` = percent,
    `Avg Distance to Polling Places (mi)`
  )


#viewing and labeling
top_critical_tracts %>%
  kable(
    digits = 1,
    caption = "Critical Tracts for Investment in Elderly Participation in Elections"
  )


Critical Tracts for Investment in Elderly Participation in Elections








Tract
Total Population
% Vulnerable
Avg Distance to Polling Places (mi)




364
1,004
49.8
1.9


220
1,477
46.6
1.9


9802
405
76.5
0.8




#mapping vulnerable tracts and hospitals for visual understanding
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = shub, fill = "white", color = "black") +
  geom_sf(data = a_vulnerable_tracts, aes(fill = percent), color = NA) +
  geom_sf(data = dhub, color = "green", size = 0.05, alpha = 0.5) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "% Age 65+", option = "plasma") +
  labs(
    title = "Polling Access vulnerability in Philadelphia",
    subtitle = "vulnerability chloropleth",
    caption = "Green points = Polling Places"
  ) +
  theme_void()







#converting critical tracts into geometric
critical_top3pls <- critical_tracts %>%
  filter(NAMELSAD10 %in% c("Census Tract 364", "Census Tract 220", "Census Tract 9802"))



ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = shub, fill = "gold", color = "gold") +
  geom_sf(data = critical_top3pls, fill = "lightblue", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_sf(data = dhub, color = "red", size = 0.05, alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Top 3 Critical Tracts for Elderly Polling Access",
    subtitle = "blue tracts are disconnected from transit and far from polling places",
    caption = "red points = Polling Places"
  ) +
  theme_void()








Philadelphia is mostly covered via transit connections, whether it be regional rail, SEPTA subway, or buses. However, gaps remain in three critical census tract areas: tracts 364, 220, and 9802. Not only do these tracts have a vulnerable population above the 20% threshold, with those aged 65 and above exceeding 45%, but the average distance to a polling place is more than a third of a mile. This is a significant concern for elderly residents, as these tracts also fall outside the 0.125-mile transit buffer entirely. While this distance may not seem substantial, most elderly individuals suffer from arthritis and knee-related issues, making walkability a critical factor. It is therefore essential that their inclusivity be prioritized.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Assignment Overview
This assignment showcases the use of regression analysis for spatial predictive analysis in the city of Chicago. Building on 311 service requests reporting vacant and abandoned buildings, we use this spatial feature to predict the likelihood of burglaries and then compare different models to determine which performs better in spatial prediction.

This assignment focuses on adapting example code from class, to analyze a new data-set while building spatial features for predictive modeling. The analysis also emphasizes interpreting and communicating model results clearly through maps as well.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Introduction
Abandoned buildings often reflect neighborhoods that have experienced disinvestment, which can gradually make them less desirable places to live. These areas may also signal limited real estate potential, contributing to environments where financially vulnerable populations are more likely to reside. Burglaries may, therefore, be one possible outcome of such conditions. Economic vulnerability can create significant stress for individuals, and vacant or abandoned buildings may serve as visible symbols of the broader economic conditions of an area. Thus, using 311 service requests reporting vacant and abandoned buildings may be an essential move for spatial predictive analysis of burglary predictions.

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
Setting Up
This section shows the setup for the analysis. Here, we load all the essential libraries that will be used throughout the analysis. Additionally, we create a theme for our visuals to customize and standardize their presentation. This is essential housekeeping before beginning the analysis.


Code
# Load required packages
library(tidyverse)      # Data manipulation
library(sf)             # Spatial operations
library(here)           # Relative file paths
library(viridis)        # Color scales
library(terra)          # Raster operations (replaces 'raster')
library(spdep)          # Spatial dependence
library(FNN)            # Fast nearest neighbors
library(MASS)           # Negative binomial regression
library(patchwork)      # Plot composition (replaces grid/gridExtra)
library(knitr)          # Tables
library(kableExtra)     # Table formatting
library(classInt)       # Classification intervals


# Spatstat for analyzing point patterns - split into sub-packages
library(spatstat.geom)    # Spatial geometries
library(spatstat.explore) # Spatial exploration/KDE

# Set options
options(scipen = 999)  # No scientific notation
set.seed(5080)         # Reproducibility, could be any number

# Create consistent theme for visualizations
theme_land <- function(base_size = 11) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 1),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(color = "gray30", size = base_size - 1),
      legend.position  = "right",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "gray92", linewidth = 0.3),
      panel.border     = element_blank(),
      axis.text        = element_blank(),
      axis.title       = element_blank(),
      axis.ticks       = element_blank()
    )
}

# Set as default
theme_set(theme_land())


cat("✓ All packages loaded successfully!\n")

title: Lab 4: Spatial Predictive Analysis
text:
ANALYSIS

1. Data Loading & Exploration
This chunk loads all the raw data required for the analysis, including both the spatial boundary files and the 311 calls dataset. The spatial boundaries are pulled from Chicago’s open data portal and include police districts, police beats, and the Chicago city boundary. Police districts are later used for cross-validation, where one district is held out at a time. Police beats represent a finer geographic unit and are also loaded, although they are not used as heavily in the later stages of the analysis. The Chicago city boundary is especially important, as it is used to clip all spatial data so that any points falling outside the city are removed.
The abandoned buildings data, which comes from a local CSV file, is then read into the analysis. Rows with missing coordinates are dropped, after which the dataset is converted into a spatial object. It is then reprojected to match the coordinate system used by the other spatial layers and clipped to the Chicago city boundary. These are all presented in the projection suitable for Chicago, ESRI:102271.


Code
policeDistricts <- 
  st_read("https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/24zt-jpfn?method=export&format=GeoJSON") %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') %>%
  dplyr::select(District = dist_num)


Reading layer `OGRGeoJSON' from data source 
  `https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/24zt-jpfn?method=export&format=GeoJSON' 
  using driver `GeoJSON'
Simple feature collection with 25 features and 2 fields
Geometry type: MULTIPOLYGON
Dimension:     XY
Bounding box:  xmin: -87.94011 ymin: 41.64455 xmax: -87.52414 ymax: 42.02303
Geodetic CRS:  WGS 84


Code
policeBeats <- 
  st_read("https://data.cityofchicago.org/api/geospatial/n9it-hstw?method=export&format=GeoJSON") %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271') %>%
  dplyr::select(Beat = beat_num)


Reading layer `OGRGeoJSON' from data source

6. Fishnet Grid Creation
The code chunk below works on creating a fishnet grid of 500m X 500m size. Fishnet grids are essential for data visualization and analysis as they are able to provide granular analyses, avoiding the bias of political boundaries. Sometimes there is variation within a larger political boundary such as a census tract or even a block group, therefore, fishnet grids help with that. Here, each cell gets a unique ID so that later you can join burglary counts and abandoned building counts onto it, essentially turning the whole city into a structured grid where every square has its own set of values to model.


Code
#creating grid over chicago boundaries
fishnet <- st_make_grid(
  chicagoBoundary,
  cellsize = 500,
  square = TRUE
) %>%
  st_sf() %>%
  mutate(uniqueID = row_number()) %>%
  st_transform('ESRI:102271')

#fixing to only be over the city boundary
fishnet <- fishnet[chicagoBoundary, ]

#using fishnet cells to attach to districts and getting rid of those that do not fit the category. This may be neeeded in cross validation later. 
fishnet <- st_join(fishnet, policeDistricts, join = st_intersects) %>%
  filter(!is.na(District))

8. Spatial Features
In this step, we calculate the k-nearest neighbors value, where k = 5, meaning the five nearest neighbors, and then find the average distance to those five nearest neighbors. This average distance will later serve as a predictor in our analysis. Here, we experiment with both 5 nearest neighbors and a second feature using 7 nearest neighbors. We find that kNN with 5 neighbors is the better predictor, based on the summary showing shorter mean and median distances, which provides a better indication of the influence of proximity.


Code
# Extract centroid coordinates of fishnet cells and point coordinates of abandoned building locations
fishnet_coords  <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(fishnet))
abandoned_coords <- st_coordinates(abandoned_sf)

# Find the 5 nearest abandoned building points for each fishnet cell
nn_result <- get.knnx(abandoned_coords, fishnet_coords, k = 5)

#second k nearest calculation to see model predictability
nn_result2 <- get.knnx(abandoned_coords, fishnet_coords, k = 7)

#assign back to fishnet 
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    abandoned.nn = rowMeans(nn_result$nn.dist)
  )

#assign back to fishnet
fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(
    abandoned7.nn = rowMeans(nn_result2$nn.dist)
  )

9. Local Moran’s I
Global Moran’s I helps determine whether an entire data-set within a given geography shows statistical evidence of spatial autocorrelation. Local Moran’s I does the same, but for local clusters within the bigger data-set. It helps identify where spatial autocorrelation exists. A high-high cluster means a high-value neighbor is surrounded by other high-value neighbors, while the opposite, low-low, means low-value neighbors are surrounded by other low-value neighbors. In this step, we code the Local Moran’s I for our fishnet.


Code
#calculating moran's i for 5 nearest neighbors (this is creating the formula)
calculate_local_morans <- function(data, variable, k = 5) {
  
  coords    <- st_coordinates(st_centroid(data))
  neighbors <- knn2nb(knearneigh(coords, k = k))
  weights   <- nb2listw(neighbors, style = "W", zero.policy = TRUE)
  
  x <- as.numeric(data[[variable]])
  
  local_moran <- localmoran(x, weights)
  
  mean_val <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  
  data %>%
    mutate(
      local_i      = local_moran[, 1],
      p_value      = local_moran[, 5],
      is_significant = p_value < 0.05,
      moran_class  = case_when(
        !is_significant ~ "Not Significant",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val  ~ "High-High",
        local_i > 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] > mean_val  ~ "High-Low",
        local_i < 0 & .data[[variable]] <= mean_val ~ "Low-High",
        TRUE ~ "Not Significant"
      )
    )
}


#applying the created formula to our context for abandoned land 
fishnet <- calculate_local_morans(fishnet, "countAbandoned", k = 5)


We calculate this to see where abandoned buildings cluster and where high-high clusters exist, so we can identify hotspots that can then be used to better understand burglary patterns. It is evident that only high values of abandonment surrounded by other high values will be of strong statistical interest here. A low-low cluster would not make much sense in this context, since we are looking for abandoned buildings and most buildings, in general, are occupied. Abandonment is a special occurrence and is therefore of high interest. However, we can also observe low-high patterns, where pockets of occupied fishnet cells are surrounded by areas with high abandonment. These will be displayed in the map below.


Code
#plotting the moran's I
ggplot() +
  geom_sf(
    data = fishnet,
    aes(fill = moran_class),
    color = NA
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "High-High"       = "#d7191c",
      "High-Low"        = "#fdae61",
      "Low-High"        = "#abd9e9",
      "Low-Low"         = "#2c7bb6",
      "Not Significant" = "gray90"
    ),
    name = "Cluster Type"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Local Moran's I: Vacant Building Clusters",
    subtitle = "High-High = Hot spots of disorder"
  ) +
  theme_land()









Now, we conduct another spatial feature engineering step. Here, we calculate the distance from hotspots to the center of each fishnet cell and then use the count of burglaries, perhaps along with distance, as a predictor. This will help us determine whether proximity to a hotspot may be associated with burglaries.


Code
#centroid of hotspot
hotspots <- fishnet %>%
  filter(moran_class == "High-High") %>%
  st_centroid()

# Calculate each cell's distance to the nearest abandoned building hot spot cluster
# If no hot spots exist, default to 0
if (nrow(hotspots) > 0) {
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(
      dist_to_hotspot = as.numeric(
        st_distance(st_centroid(fishnet), hotspots %>% st_union())
      )
    )
  cat("✓ Calculated distance to vacant building hot spots\n")
  cat("  - Number of hot spot cells:", nrow(hotspots), "\n")
} else {
  fishnet <- fishnet %>%
    mutate(dist_to_hotspot = 0)
  cat("⚠ No significant hot spots found\n")
}

  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::select(

    abandoned.nn,

All three predictors are highly significant, with p-values below 0.001. countAbandoned has a positive coefficient, suggesting that as the number of abandoned buildings in a cell increases, the expected count of burglaries also increases. abandoned.nn has a negative coefficient, meaning that as the distance from abandoned buildings increases, the expected number of burglaries decreases. Similarly, dist_to_hotspot is also negative, suggesting that cells located farther from hotspot clusters tend to have fewer burglaries.

12. Spatial Cross-Validation
We hold one district out at a time, fit the model on the rest, and then predict for that held-out district. This process is repeated until every district has served as the test set once, helping us understand how well the model generalizes to completely new spatial areas. We use the Leave-One-Group-Out cross validation, by holding out entire police districts at a time, we ensure that the test area is truly spatially separated from the training data. This gives a more honest picture of how the model would perform when applied to a new area it has not seen before. We then calculate the Mean Absolute Error and the Root Mean Square Error. These are tests for the predictive accuracy of the model. Lower the RMSE the better the model predictions.


Code
districts  <- unique(fishnet_model$District)
cv_results <- tibble()

cat("Running LOGO Cross-Validation...\n")


Running LOGO Cross-Validation...


Code
#the cross validatyion model 
for (i in seq_along(districts)) {
  
  test_district <- districts[i]
  
  train_data <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District != test_district)
  test_data  <- fishnet_model %>% filter(District == test_district)
  
  model_cv <- glm.nb(
    countBurglaries ~ countAbandoned + abandoned.nn + dist_to_hotspot,
    data = train_data
  )
  
  test_data <- test_data %>%
    mutate(prediction = predict(model_cv, test_data, type = "response"))
  
  mae  <- mean(abs(test_data$countBurglaries - test_data$prediction))
  rmse <- sqrt(mean((test_data$countBurglaries - test_data$prediction)^2))
  
  cv_results <- bind_rows(
    cv_results,
    tibble(
      fold          = i,
      test_district = test_district,
      n_test        = nrow(test_data),
      mae           = mae,
      rmse          = rmse
    )
  )
  
  cat("  Fold", i, "/", length(districts), "- District", test_district,
      "- MAE:", round(mae, 2), "\n")
}%>%
  kable()

  )

Code
#Scale KDE values to match the total burglary count, so that KDE and the negative binomial predictions are on the same scale for fair comparison
kde_sum   <- sum(fishnet$kde_value,       na.rm = TRUE)
count_sum <- sum(fishnet$countBurglaries, na.rm = TRUE)

fishnet <- fishnet %>%
  mutate(prediction_kde = (kde_value / kde_sum) * count_sum)


#visualize burglaries actual, Negative Binomial Model, and KDE
m1 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = countBurglaries), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Count", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "Actual Burglaries") +
  theme_land()

m2 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_nb), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "Model Predictions (NB)") +
  theme_land()

m3 <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data = fishnet, aes(fill = prediction_kde), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Predicted", option = "plasma", limits = c(0, 15)) +
  labs(title = "KDE Baseline Predictions") +
  theme_land()

m1 + m2 + m3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title    = "Actual vs. Predicted Burglaries",
    subtitle = "Does our complex model outperform simple KDE?"
  )









Visually, all three look similar, therefore a more robust comparison is required.


Code
comparison <- fishnet %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  filter(!is.na(prediction_nb), !is.na(prediction_kde)) %>%
  summarize(
    model_mae  = mean(abs(countBurglaries - prediction_nb)),
    model_rmse = sqrt(mean((countBurglaries - prediction_nb)^2)),
    kde_mae    = mean(abs(countBurglaries - prediction_kde)),
    kde_rmse   = sqrt(mean((countBurglaries - prediction_kde)^2))
  )

comparison %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  separate(metric, into = c("approach", "metric"), sep = "_") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = value) %>%
  kable(
    digits  = 2,
    caption = "Model Performance Comparison"
  ) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"))

To conclude, we can see that the KDE performs better than the model itself, with lower MAE and RMSE values. While the Negative Binomial regression is technically more robust, the possibility of missing spatial variables beyond those included in the model may be one reason for this weaker performance.
